1: ʾĕlōhîm bāʾû gôyim bĕnaḥălātekhā — O God, the nations have invaded your inheritance. Naḥălāh (inheritance, possession) — the land is God's property, not Israel's; the invasion is a property violation against God, not merely a national disaster. ṭimmĕʾû ʾet-hêkhal qodshekā — they have defiled your holy temple. Ṭāmēʾ (piel: to defile, make ritually impure) — the same defilement language used of corpse-impurity in Num 19 applied to the sanctuary. The temple cannot function when defiled; God's dwelling place is rendered uninhabitable.
2: nātĕnû ʾet-nĕblat ʿăbādêkhā maʾăkhāl lĕʿôph hashshāmāyim — they have given the corpses of your servants as food to the birds of the heavens. Nĕblāh (corpse, carcass — the term for an unburied body, often used of animals; its application to humans marks the utter dehumanization). Unburied corpses were the supreme disgrace in ancient Mediterranean culture (cf. Tobit's dangerous act of burying corpses; Antigone's resistance). bĕśar ḥăsîdêkhā lĕḥayyat ʾāreṣ — the flesh of your godly ones to the beasts of the earth. Ḥāsîd (the pious, the faithful, the covenant-loyal ones) — those who embodied covenant loyalty are now carrion. The implicit appeal: God's own faithful deserve better.
3: shāpĕkhû dāmām kĕmayim sĕbîbôt yĕrûshālāim — they have poured out their blood like water all around Jerusalem. Shāphak dām (to pour out blood — the language of murder and of sacrifice, sometimes overlapping) + kĕmayim (like water — the comparison emphasizes both quantity and the casual disregard, as though blood were as cheap as water). wĕʾên qôbēr — and there was no one to bury the dead. The absence of burial is the final detail of shame — the dead lie exposed because there are no survivors with the capacity or freedom to bury them.
4: hāyînû ḥerpāh lishkhēnênû — we have become a reproach to our neighbors. Ḥerpāh (reproach, disgrace — the social-shame vocabulary of failed covenant loyalty) — national defeat is experienced as public humiliation before neighboring peoples. laʿag wāqelesleṣ lisĕbîbôtênû — a mockery and derision to those around us. Two terms for ridicule (laʿag: mockery, contemptuous laughter; qelesleṣ: derision, scoffing) — the nations surrounding Israel find the defeat not tragic but comic, a confirmation of their low estimation of YHWH's power.
5: ʿad-māh YHWH tĕʾĕnap lāneṣaḥ — how long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever? The ʿad-māh (how long?) formula is the communal lament's cry for divine action — not a complaint that God is wrong but a plea that the duration of his silence/anger is unbearable. tiʿar kaʾesh qinʾātekhā — will your jealousy burn like fire? Qinʾāh (jealousy — the divine jealousy provoked by Israel's covenant-breaking now burning against Israel; the psalmist is aware that the destruction is covenantally earned). The appeal is not that God is unjust but that the punishment is sufficient.
6: shĕphōkh ḥămātĕkhā ʾel-haggôyim ʾăsher lōʾ yĕdāʿûkhā — pour out your wrath on the nations that do not know you. Shāphak ḥĕmāh (to pour out fury) — the same cup-of-wrath imagery (cf. Psalms 75:8; Jeremiah 10:25 quotes this almost verbatim). The argument: if the jealousy-anger is burning (v5), redirect it toward those who deserve it — the nations that never acknowledged YHWH. wĕʿal mamlakyôt ʾăsher bĕshimĕkhā lōʾ qārāʾû — and on the kingdoms that do not call on your name. The criterion is worship — those who do not call on the divine name versus Israel who does (or should).
7: kî ʾākal ʾet-yaʿăqōb — for they have devoured Jacob. ʾĀkal (to eat, devour — used of nations devouring Israel as a predator devours prey, cf. Jeremiah 10:25 where this verse is nearly quoted). wĕʾet-nāwēhû heshammû — and laid waste his homeland. Nāweh (home, pasture, pleasant habitation) — Jacob's pleasant home reduced to shāmam (desolation). The homeland as both habitat and theological inheritance is destroyed.
8: ʾal-tizkār-lānû ʿăwōnōt rîshōnîm — do not hold our former iniquities against us. Rîshōnîm (former, earlier) — possibly the sins of previous generations (ancestral guilt as the cause of the current punishment), or the psalmist's own past sins. The prayer is explicitly penitential — unusual in communal laments which more often assert innocence. mahēr yĕqaddĕmûnû raḥămêkhā — let your compassion come quickly to meet us. Qādaph (piel: to come before, to meet — to intercept on the way) — the compassion racing ahead of further punishment to intercept the people before they are destroyed. kî dallônû meʾōd — for we are utterly brought low. Dal (thin, weak, impoverished) — the confession of total destitution.
9: ʿozrênû ʾĕlōhê yishʿēnû ʿal-dĕbar kĕbôd-shĕmekā — help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of your name. ʾĔlōhê yishʿēnû (God of our salvation) — the name invoked contains the plea; the God whose name is salvation is asked to act consistently with his own name. The motivation is explicit: ʿal-dĕbar kĕbôd-shĕmekā (for the sake of the glory of your name) — not Israel's merit but divine reputation. wĕhaṣṣîlênû wĕkhappēr ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾtênû lĕmaʿan shĕmekā — deliver us and forgive our sins, for your name's sake. The name-argument appears twice — the governing motivation throughout: God's action in history displays his character to the watching nations.
10: lāmmāh yōʾmĕrû haggôyim ʾayyēh ʾĕlōhêhem — why should the nations say, 'Where is their God?' The taunt of the nations (cf. Psalms 42:3,10; Psalms 115:2; Joel 2:17) — the defeat of Israel is interpreted internationally as the defeat of YHWH. The question ʾayyēh ʾĕlōhêhem (where is their God?) is the practical atheism of military victory: if YHWH cannot protect his people, he has no effective power. yiwwādaʿ baggôyim lĕʿênênû niqmat dam-ʿăbādêkhā hashshāphûkh — before our eyes, let the avenging of your servants' shed blood be known among the nations. The vengeance for blood is a covenant obligation (Genesis 9:5-6; Deuteronomy 32:43) — God owes the blood of his servants an answer.
11: tābôʾ lĕphānêkhā ʾanqat ʾāsîr — let the groaning of the prisoner come before you. ʾAnqāh (groaning — the low, involuntary sound of one in pain or imprisonment; used of oppressed Israel in Egypt, Exodus 2:24) — the prisoners' groaning as wordless prayer reaching the divine ears. kĕgōdel zĕrôʿăkhā hôtēr bĕnê tĕmûtāh — according to your great power, preserve those condemned to die. Bĕnê tĕmûtāh (sons of death — those legally sentenced to die, or those who will certainly die; a legal idiom for the condemned) — the appeal is for divine commutation of the death sentence imposed by the enemy. Zĕrôaʿ (arm) — the Exodus arm (Psalms 77:15) invoked again.
12: wĕhāshēb lishkhēnênû shebʿātayim ʾel-ḥêqām — return to our neighbors sevenfold into their bosom the reproach. Shebʿātayim (sevenfold — not literally seven times but the full intensified repayment, as in the Cain-Lamech sequence of Genesis 4:15,24) — the complete, comprehensive vengeance that exceeds proportional retaliation. Ḥêq (bosom, lap — to pour into the bosom is to give someone what they deserve, cf. Isaiah 65:6-7). ḥerpātām ʾăsher ḥērĕphûkhā ʾădōnāy — the reproach with which they have taunted you, O Lord. The reproach is directed at YHWH, not merely at Israel — the motivation for its repayment is again divine honor.
13: waʾănaḥnû ʿamĕkhā wĕṣōʾn marʿîtekhā — then we your people, the sheep of your pasture. The closing self-identification: ʿamĕkhā (your people) + ṣōʾn marʿîtekhā (the flock of your pasture) — both the national covenant language and the shepherd-flock metaphor of Psalms 78:52,71-72. The nation that was led from Egypt like a flock claims the same identity in exile: still the shepherd's flock. nôdeh lĕkhā lĕʿôlām lĕdôr wādôr nĕsappēr tĕhillātekhā — will give you thanks forever; from generation to generation we will recount your praise. The promised praise provides a future purpose for present rescue: God rescues so that his people can praise. The generational praise-chain of Psalms 78:1-8 returns as the goal toward which Ps 79 points.
